questions.explanation: Looking at the diagram, it is apparent that increasing glucose concentration in the surrounding tube will decrease the difference in the concentration of glucose between the surrounding liquid and inside the visking tubing. As there is less concentration gradient, the net diffusion of water would decrease so statement I is incorrect. The time to reach equilibrium would decrease as there is less difference in concentration of glucose, so statement II is correct. As the concentration gradient is less, less net movement of water would be needed to reach equilibrium, so volume would also change less, hence statement III is correct. There is no movement of glucose molecules, so statement IV is incorrect.

questions.text: In which type of cells, cell wall is not present?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plant cells have a cell wall made of cellulose, which provides rigidity and support.
Fungal cells have a cell wall made of chitin, which gives structure and protection.
Bacterial cells have a cell wall made of peptidoglycan (in most bacteria), providing strength and shape.
Liver cells (hepatocytes) are animal cells, and animal cells do not have a cell wall. They only have a cell membrane for protection and structure.

questions.explanation: A cell wall is a structural layer surrounding some types of cells, just outside the cell membrane. It can be tough, flexible, and sometimes rigid. It provides the cell with both structural support and protection, and also acts as a filtering mechanism. No cells in the human body have a cell wall, hence liver cells will not have one either. Plant cell walls are primarily made of cellulose, which is the most abundant macromolecule on Earth. The cell wall is a characteristic structure of fungi and is composed mainly of glucans, chitin, and glycoproteins. The bacterial cell wall consists of peptidoglycan, an essential protective barrier for bacterial cells that encapsulates the cytoplasmic membrane.

questions.text: 70S sized ribosomes are found in the cytoplasm of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AlgaeAlgae are eukaryotic organisms and have 80S ribosomes in their cytoplasm.However, chloroplasts in algae contain 70S ribosomes, similar to bacteria.
FungiFungi (e.g., molds, yeast) are eukaryotic and have 80S ribosomes in their cytoplasm.
ProtozoansProtozoa are eukaryotic unicellular organisms and have 80S ribosomes in their cytoplasm.

The correct answer is:BacteriaExplanation:70S ribosomes are found in prokaryotic cells, including bacteria and archaea.The S (Svedberg unit) refers to the sedimentation rate of ribosomes during centrifugation, not their actual size.

questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells have mitochondria and chloroplasts as organelles and those organelles additionally have ribosomes 70S. However, eukaryotic cells (algae, fungi, protozoans) have 80S ribosomes in their cytoplasm, while prokaryotic cells just have 70S ribosomes. Hence, Option D is correct.

questions.text: Tissue is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A tissue is a group of similar cells that work together to perform a specific function. Examples of tissues include Muscle tissue (made of muscle cells, which help in movement). Nervous tissue (made of nerve cells, transmits signals), Epithelial tissue (covers body surfaces and organs), Connective tissue (supports and connects different body parts, like bone and blood)

Group of Organs → Organ SystemAn organ system consists of multiple organs working together to perform a vital function.Example: The digestive system includes the stomach, intestines, and liver, all working to process food

Group of Tissues → OrganAn organ is made up of different tissues working together for a specific function.Example: The heart has muscle tissue, connective tissue, and nervous tissue, all working together to pump blood

A group of organisms is called a population.Explanation:A population consists of organisms of the same species living together in a specific area.Example: A group of zebras in a grassland is a population of zebras.

questions.explanation: Tissue is a group of cells that have similar structure and that function together as a unit.

questions.text: Suggest which among the following is not a function attributed to endoplasmic reticulum?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum is involved in the detoxification of the poisons and the drugs. This is the main function of the endoplasmic reticulum in the liver. The manufacturing of the lipids and fat molecules take place in the smooth ER. The membrane proteins are synthesized on the ribosomes which are present on the surface of the ER.

options.text: Digestion/egestion of foreign materials outside the cell
options.explanation: It is done by lysosomes.The digestion and Egestion of the foreign materials which enter the cells take place in the lysosomes. Thus, the correct answer is option B.

options.text: Manufacture of fat and lipid molecules
options.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum is involved in the detoxification of the poisons and the drugs. This is the main function of the endoplasmic reticulum in the liver. The manufacturing of the lipids and fat molecules take place in the smooth ER. The membrane proteins are synthesized on the ribosomes which are present on the surface of the ER.

The endoplasmic reticulum is involved in the detoxification of the poisons and the drugs. This is the main function of the endoplasmic reticulum in the liver. The manufacturing of the lipids and fat molecules take place in the smooth ER. The membrane proteins are synthesized on the ribosomes which are present on the surface of the ER.

questions.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum is involved in the detoxification of the poisons and the drugs. This is the main function of the endoplasmic reticulum in the liver. The manufacturing of the lipids and fat molecules take place in the smooth ER. The membrane proteins are synthesised on the ribosomes which are present on the surface of the ER. The digestion and egestion of the foreign materials which enter the cells take place in the lysosomes. Thus, the correct answer is option B.

questions.text: Which of the following cell organelles involved in the synthesis of cell wall and middle lamella?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: SER is involved in lipid and carbohydrate synthesis while RERis involved in protein synthesis.
It is involved in cell wall formation through phragmoplast which is formed by golgi vesicles.
It has digestive and hydrolytic enzymes.
It is involved in decomposition and formation of hydrogen peroxide.

questions.explanation: The Golgi and plasma membranes are the two main sites where constituents of cell wall are synthesized. The Golgi body is also known as Golgi apparatus. It synthesizes vesicles which then fuse to form the middle lamella as shown below.
hints: synthesis of cell wall and middle lamella

questions.text: It is the non-living component of the cell, it is secreted and maintained by the living portion of the cell and is found only in plants. What is this structure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is a living organelle involved in modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids for secretion or use within the cell. It is found in both plant and animal cells.
Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles that contain enzymes for digesting cellular waste and other substances. They are more commonly found in animal cells and are living components.
Mitochondria are energy-producing organelles found in both plant and animal cells. They are involved in cellular respiration and are living components.

The cell wall is a non-living structure that surrounds the cell membrane in plant cells. It provides structural support and protection. It is secreted by the cell and is primarily composed of cellulose in plants.
Ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis in cells and are found in both plant and animal cells. They are living components and essential for translating genetic information into proteins.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the cell wall, which is a non-living component unique to plant cells among the options provided. It is secreted and maintained by the living parts of the cell, such as the plasma membrane and the Golgi apparatus. The cell wall provides structure, protection, and supports the plant cell against mechanical stress and osmotic pressure. It is primarily composed of cellulose in plants.Other options such as the Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, mitochondria, and ribosomes are all living organelles involved in various cell functions and are found in both plant and animal cells. They do not serve the structural role that the cell wall does.

questions.text: The diagram shows some similarities between golgi apparatus, mitochondria and suicide sacs. Which one is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Though golgi does release packaged vesicles, which are further modified into proteins, the mitochondria do not release any enzymes.
Suicide sacs and mitochondria do contain a membrane. Though golgi does release packaged vesicles, which are further modified into proteins, the mitochondria do not release any enzymes.
Suicide sacs and golgi do have a membrane.
Suicide sacs, Golgi, and mitochondria are all membrane-bound organelles.

questions.explanation: Option D is the only option that makes sense. Suicide sacs are lysosomal enzymes that are membrane bound. Mitochondria and golgi apparatus are also membrane bounded organelles.

questions.text: Which of the following is correct for the given structure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: These are small structures which work like oars
options.explanation: Cilia and flagella are hair-like outgrowths of the cell membrane. Cilia are small structures working like oars, causing the movement of either the cell or the surrounding fluid. Flagella, in comparison, is longer and is responsible for cell movement. Both cilium and flagellum are covered with plasma membrane. Their core, called axoneme, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis.

Flagella, in comparison, is longer and is responsible for cell movement. Both cilium and flagellum are covered with plasma membrane. Their core, called axoneme, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis.

Cilia and flagella are hair-like outgrowths of the cell membrane. Cilia are small structures working like oars, causing the movement of either the cell or the surrounding fluid. Flagella, in comparison, is longer and is responsible for cell movement. Both cilium and flagellum are covered with plasma membrane. Their core, called axoneme, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis.

Flagella, in comparison, is longer and is responsible for cell movement. Both cilium and flagellum are covered with plasma membrane. Their core, called axoneme, possesses a number of microtubules running parallel to the long axis.

questions.text: Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct regarding a nucleus? I.Stores wastes and other SubstancesII.Contains genetic materialIII.Helps in cellular transport systemIV.Control centre of the cell
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nucleus doesn't store wastes and any other kind of substances. Nucleus is the governor house of cell. It controls the life and activities of cells.
Nucleus stores genetic material but doesn't store any kind of wastes . So , statement II makes option B incorrect.
Nucleus doesn't help in cellular transport system. So , this option is incorrect.
Nucleus is the control centre of the cell and stores the genetic material of the cell,so we say that statements II and IV are correct, making option D correct.
Nucleus is the control center of cell but does not help in cellular respiration. Thus , statement III makes option E incorrect.

questions.explanation: Nucleus is the control centre of the cell and stores the genetic material of the cell,so we say that statements II and IV are correct, making option D correct. Nucleus does not store wastes and other substances plus it also does not help in cellular transport system, that eliminate option A,B,C and E.

questions.text: Which features do animal cells share with plant cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is wrong as it shows that chloroplast is also shared among plant and animal cells, which is incorrect. Chlorophyll is only present in plant cells not in animal cells.
Option B is wrong as it shows that cytoplasm is not shared among plant and animal cells, which is wrong.
Mitochondria, cytoplasm and nucleus are found in both plant and animal cells. However, chloroplast is the organelle found in plant cells but lacking in animal cells
Option D shows that only mitochondria is the shared organelle, not the nucleus and cytoplasm, that makes it incorrect.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria, cytoplasm and nucleus are found in both plant and animal cells. However, chloroplast is the organelle found in plant cells but lacking in animal cells.

questions.text: Its membranes are the sites where sunlight energy is trapped and where glucose is formed. Which organelle is this?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is correct option because glucose is produced from photosynthesis.
Option B is wrong because Leucoplasts are important organelles for the synthesis and storage of starch, lipids and proteins.

Option C is wrong because Chromoplasts are plastids and contain carotenoids. They lack chlorophyll. Carotenoid pigments are responsible for different colours like yellow, orange and red colour imparted to fruits, flowers, old leaves, roots, etc. Chromoplasts may develop from green chloroplasts.

Option D is wrong because Cytosol gives internal support to cell; Cytosol contains many molecules needed by cell for metabolism. Structure: Microfilaments and microtubules forming a network throughout the cytoplasm.

questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are plant cell organelles that convert light energy into relatively stable chemical energy via the photosynthetic process.

questions.text: Anthocyanins are various types of colorful pigments present in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is correct because A photosynthetic pigment is a pigment that is present in chloroplasts or photosynthetic bacteria and captures the light energy necessary for photosynthesis.

Option B is wrong because Chromoplasts synthesize and store pigments such as orange carotene, yellow xanthophylls, and various other red pigments. As such, their color varies depending on what pigment they contain.
Option C is wrong because Leucoplasts are important organelles for the synthesis and storage of starch, lipids and proteins.They are color less pigments.
D is the correct option because Anthocyanins are flavonoid pigments that accumulate in the large central vacuole of most plants.

questions.explanation: Anthocyanins are found in the cell vacuole, mostly in flowers and fruits, but also in leaves, stems, and roots

questions.text: Tay Sach's disease is caused by absence of enzyme required to breakdown _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is correct option because the genetic change that causes Tay-Sachs disease results in a deficiency of the enzyme beta-hexosaminidase A. This enzyme is required to break down the fatty substance lipids . The buildup of fatty substances damages nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord.
Option B is wrong because glycogen storage disease is caused due to absence of enzymes required to break glycogen.
Option C is wrong because von Gierke disease is caused due to absence of enzymes required to break glucose.
Option D is wrong because Urea cycle disorder is caused due to absence of enzymes required to break protein.

questions.explanation: Tay-Sachs disease is a inherited disorder that progressively destroys nerve cells (neurons) in the brain and spinal cord. It is caused due to the mutation in the gene which is responsible for producing enzyme involved break down of fatty substance.

questions.text: The process of membrane fusion and the movement of material out of the cell, is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phagocytosis is the process of a cell engulfing large particles or cells, forming an internal vesicle known as a phagosome. It is a type of endocytosis, not related to exporting materials.
Endocytosis involves the cellular process of taking substances into the cell. This process is the opposite of exocytosis.
Pinocytosis is a form of endocytosis where cells ingest extracellular fluids and small molecules. It is not involved in the export of materials from the cell.

Exocytosis is the process where secretory vesicles merge with the plasma membrane, releasing their contents to the external environment. This process is essential for removing waste and secreting substances.

questions.explanation: The process of moving substances out of the cell is called exocytosis.Endocytosis is moving substances into the cell.Phagocytosis is movement of substances into the cell which are solid and massive.Pinocytosis is movement of substances into the cell in liquid state.

questions.text: The diagram is taken from an electron micrograph of a cell, name the organelle labelled B:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus is typically a large, round organelle that contains genetic material, not a series of flattened sacs like the Golgi complex.
The Golgi complex is identified by its stacked, flattened membrane-bound sacs, which are responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins.
Lysosomes are small, spherical organelles containing digestive enzymes, unlike the stacked structure of the Golgi complex.
Mitochondria have a double membrane with inner folds known as cristae, which differ from the flattened sacs of the Golgi complex.
Vacuoles are large, fluid-filled organelles that maintain cell pressure and storage, not the structured appearance of a Golgi complex.

questions.explanation: Organelle labelled B is Golgi complex.

questions.text: Founder of cell biology is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In 1839, Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann proposed the cell theory that all living organisms are composed of one or more cells, and that the cell is the basic unit of life.
In 1858, Rudolf Virchow suggested that new cells arise only from pre-existing cells, a principle known as biogenesis.

The history of cell biology dates back to the early seventeenth century when Robert Hooke discovered the cell in 1665. He observed a slice of cork under a microscope and found empty chambers resembling the structure of a honeycomb. He called these chambers "cells" as they reminded him of prison cells.
Robert Brown discovered the cell nucleus in 1833, and in 1879, Walther Flemming observed chromosomes during cell division, which led to the discovery of the process of mitosis.

questions.explanation: This is purely factual recall. The cell was first discovered and named by Robert Hooke in 1665.

questions.text: Elaioplasts found in plants are actually one of the types of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Elaioplasts found in plants are actually one of the types of plastids but more specifically leucoplasts..
Chromoplasts are plastids that are associtaed with bright colours.
Elaioplasts found in plants are actually one of the types of leucoplasts.
Elaioplasts found in plants are actually one of the types of leucoplasts.

questions.explanation: Elaioplasts found in plants are actually one of the types of leucoplasts.

questions.text: Chief material present in the cell wall of plants, fungal, and prokaryotic cells is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are essential constituents of plant cell walls; they are involved in modifications of cell wall components, wall structure, signaling, and interactions with plasma membrane proteins at the cell surface.
Lipids are found primarily in bacterial cell walls. They maintain a selective permeability for the import and export of water-soluble compounds in the bacteria.

The polysaccharide found in plants including hemicellulose, pectin, and cellulose. In fungi, chitin is found which is also a polysaccharide. Bacterial cell walls consist mainly of the polysaccharide i.e., peptidoglycan.

Phospholipids are major membrane lipids that consist of lipid bilayers. This basic cellular structure acts as a barrier to protect the cell against various environmental insults and more importantly, enables multiple cellular processes to occur in subcellular compartments.

questions.explanation: The primary cell wall of land plants is composed of polysaccharides, cellulose, hemicelluloses, and pectin.

questions.text: Which type of leucoplasts store lipids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amyloplasts store starch.
Leucoplasts are classified into three groups: amyloplasts, elaioplasts or osteoblasts, and proteinoplasts. Elaioplasts store lipids.
Proteinoplasts store proteins.
Etioplasts are photosynthetically inactive plastids that accumulate when light levels are too low for chloroplast maturation.

questions.explanation: Leucoplasts are classified into three groups: amyloplasts (which store starch), elaioplasts or osteoblasts (store lipids), and proteinoplasts (store proteins).

questions.text: Which type of movement through the cell membrane is not an energy-consuming process?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the cytoplasm, macromolecules or large particles are moved across the plasma membrane by vesicles. Endocytosis and exocytosis are examples of vesicle transport. They are both active, energy-consuming processes.

In the cytoplasm, macromolecules or large particles are moved across the plasma membrane by vesicles. Endocytosis and exocytosis are examples of vesicle transport. They are both active, energy-consuming processes.
Active transport is the transport of molecules that require the use of energy, in the form of an energy-storing chemical called ATP (adenosine triphosphate) to help them cross the cell membrane.

Osmosis is a form of passive transport when water molecules move from low solute concentration (high water concentration) to high solute or low water concentration across a membrane that is not permeable to the solute.

questions.explanation: 'Osmosis' is the movement of a solvent across a semipermeable membrane, toward a higher concentration of solute (lower concentration of solvent). Both 'diffusion' and 'osmosis' are passive transport processes, which means they do not require any input of extra energy to occur.

questions.text: Fibers of the extracellular matrix are attached to_ plasma membrane:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phospholipids provide the structural framework of the plasma membrane, forming a bilayer that separates the cell from its surroundings. However, they do not directly interact with the extracellular matrix fibers.
Glycolipids, which consist of a carbohydrate attached to a lipid, are involved in cell recognition and communication but do not serve as attachment points for extracellular matrix fibers.
Proteins, specifically integrins, are the correct answer. These transmembrane proteins connect the extracellular matrix to the cell's cytoskeleton, facilitating communication and structural support.

Carbohydrates are found on the exterior of the plasma membrane, attached to proteins or lipids. They play a role in cell signaling and adhesion but are not the main components for attaching extracellular matrix fibers.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Proteins. The fibers of the extracellular matrix attach to proteins known as integrins within the plasma membrane. Integrins span the membrane and connect to both extracellular matrix proteins and intracellular cytoskeletal proteins, enabling the cell to transmit signals and maintain its structure. Phospholipids form the bilayer of the membrane but are not directly involved in binding the matrix fibers. Glycolipids, with their role in cell recognition, and Carbohydrates, involved in signaling, do not serve as attachment points for the extracellular matrix.

questions.text: _ organelles are involved in the synthesis of the plant cell wall.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ER is involved in cell to cell communication via the plasmodesmata, and, in specialized cells, it serves as a storage site for proteins. The plant ER is also equipped with enzymes and structural proteins which are involved in the process of oil body biogenesis and lipid storage.

In plant cells, the Golgi apparatus further serves as the site at which the complex polysaccharides of the cell wall are synthesized. Golgi Apparatus is the organelle that are involved in the synthesis of the plant cell wall.
Spherosomes (or Oleosomes) are single membrane-bound cell organelles that are found only in plant cells. It is also called plant lysosome.
In plant cells, peroxisomes play vital roles in metabolism, since they house many processes including fatty acid β-oxidation, glyoxylate cycles in seedlings, and photorespiration in leaves.

questions.explanation: The Golgi and plasma membranes are the two main sites, where constituents of the cell wall are synthesized.
hints: synthesis of the plant cell wall.

questions.text: Select the pair of organs, that contains a large number of mitochondria:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the liver contains a large number of mitochondria, the stomach does not have as many as the liver. The stomach's energy requirements are not as high as those of other organs known for high mitochondrial density.
Muscle cells contain a large number of mitochondria due to their high energy requirements, but the stomach does not share this characteristic to the same extent.
Both the heart and liver have a high number of mitochondria, but the heart has an even greater density due to its constant need for energy to pump blood.
Liver cells and muscle cells indeed contain a lot of mitochondria due to their energy requirements; however, the heart has a higher concentration compared to the liver.

Both the heart and muscle tissues require a substantial amount of energy for their functions, resulting in a high number of mitochondria in their cells. The heart is particularly noted for its high mitochondrial density, essential for its continuous pumping action.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Heart and Muscle. Both the heart and muscle tissues require a significant amount of energy due to their continuous and vigorous activities. The heart, in particular, is constantly pumping blood throughout the body, necessitating a high number of mitochondria for energy production. Muscle cells also need a lot of energy for movement and thus have many mitochondria. The other options, such as the liver, although rich in mitochondria, do not match the heart's concentration. The stomach does not have as high energy demands as these organs.

questions.text: Which of the following cells does not have a nucleus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscle cells have a nucleus per cell. Different types of muscle cells have different numbers of nuclei. For example: Skeletal muscle cells are long, cylindrical, and striated. They are multinucleated meaning that they have more than one nucleus.

The Nucleus of a neuron is an oval-shaped membrane-bound structure found in the soma or body of the neuron. It contains the nucleolus and chromosomes, necessary for the coded production of proteins within the cell. The nucleolus of the nucleus produces ribosomes.
White blood cells, on the other hand, are actively working to help protect the body from infections and they need to be able to make proteins to do this and therefore must keep their nuclei.

Unlike most other eukaryotic cells, mature red blood cells don't have nuclei. When they enter the bloodstream for the first time, they eject their nuclei and organelles so they can carry more hemoglobin, and thus, more oxygen. Mature red blood cells do not need to have a nucleus because they have already made all the proteins they will ever need.

questions.explanation: Unlike most other eukaryotic cells, mature red blood cells don't have nuclei. When they enter the bloodstream for the first time, they eject their nuclei and organelles so, they can carry more hemoglobin, and thus, more oxygen. Each red blood cell has a lifespan of around 100–120 days.

questions.text: The basic structural framework of all types of membranes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phospholipids are major membrane lipids that consist of lipid bilayers. This basic cellular structure acts as a barrier to protect the cell against various environmental insults and more importantly, enables multiple cellular processes to occur in subcellular compartments.

Glycoproteins act as cell surface markers. Glycoproteins perform vital biochemical and structural functions. They enable cells to recognize another cell as familiar or foreign, which is called cell-cell recognition. They also help cells attach to and bind to other cells, which is called cell adhesion.

Lipoproteins are formed by the combination of lipids and proteins and serve as the basic structural framework of all types of membranes in the cells. Lipoproteins help maintain the lipid composition of cell membranes..
Nucleoproteins are important for the structure and function of the cell nucleus. They are responsible for the transport of genetic material within the nucleus.

questions.explanation: Lipoproteins formed by the combination of lipids and proteins serve as the basic structural framework of all types of membranes in the cells.

questions.explanation: Resolution is the measurement of the number of pixelsNaked eye resolution is 576 Megapixels which is 500 times the resolution of compound microscopeThe resolution of the naked eye refers to its ability to distinguish and perceive fine details or distinguish between two closely spaced objects. The resolution of the human eye is limited by several factors, including the number of photoreceptor cells in the retina and the optics of the eye.On average, the human eye can resolve objects that are approximately 1 arcminute apart. This means that if two objects are separated by an angle of 1 arcminute (which is 1/60th of a degree), a person with normal vision would be able to perceive them as separate entities rather than a single object.

questions.text: Which one of the following combinations is NOT part of an animal cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes and cell membrane are present in animal cell but central vacuole is not
chloroplast is not present in animal cell but ribosomes and mitochondria are

Cell wall, chloroplast, central vacuole all three are not a part of the animal cellChatGPTIn animal cells, vacuoles are membrane-bound organelles that serve various functions depending on the type of animal and the specific cell type. While vacuoles are more prominent and diverse in plant cells, animal cells also possess certain types of vacuoles with specialized roles. Here are a few examples:Contractile Vacuoles: These vacuoles are found in certain freshwater unicellular organisms, such as Paramecium, and aid in osmoregulation. They regulate the water balance by actively pumping out excess water that enters the cell through osmosis.Food Vacuoles: In protozoans and certain types of animal cells, food vacuoles are formed during the process of phagocytosis. They engulf and digest food particles, breaking them down into simpler forms that can be utilized by the cell.Central Vacuole in some specialized cells: Certain animal cells, such as those found in sponges or some coelenterates (like hydras), may possess central vacuoles. These vacuoles function in regulating hydrostatic pressure, providing support, and aiding in buoyancy control.

Ribosomes and mitochondria are present in animal cell but cell wall is notcell wall is a rigid structure found in plant cells, bacteria, fungi, and some protists. It is a tough and protective layer located outside the cell membrane. While the cell wall serves important functions in organisms that possess it, animal cells do not have a cell wall. Here are a few reasons why animal cells lack

questions.explanation: Animal cells do not have a Cell wall and Chloroplasts. They have temporary small vacuoles; unlike plant cells which have large, permanent, central vacuoles.In the other options, at least one feature is present in the animal cells instead of all the three features pertaining to animal cells. The idea is to choose the better answer.The diagram presented below shows the major organelles in an animal cell; Flexibility and Mobility: Animal cells are often required to move and change shape, which is facilitated by their flexible cell membrane. The absence of a rigid cell wall allows animal cells to have more flexibility and mobility compared to cells with a cell wall. This flexibility is essential for various cellular processes, including cell migration, muscle contraction, and the ability to form complex tissues and organs.Intercellular Communication: Animal cells rely on direct cell-to-cell communication through specialized structures like gap junctions and tight junctions. These junctions facilitate the exchange of signals, ions, and molecules between neighboring cells, allowing for coordinated responses and tissue organization. A cell wall would hinder the direct interaction between animal cells and impede the efficient communication required for their complex physiological processes.Structural Support: In the absence of a cell wall, animal cells utilize the cytoskeleton, a network of protein filaments, to maintain their structural integrity and provide support. The cytoskeleton allows animal cells to maintain their shape, organize their internal components, and carry out cellular functions. This dynamic framework provides mechanical strength while allowing flexibility and shape changes.Adaptation to Environment: Animals have evolved to inhabit diverse environments and face varying physical conditions. The absence of a rigid cell wall enables animal cells to adapt to different osmotic pressures, temperature changes, and other environmental factors. Animal cells can regulate their volume and shape in response to external stimuli, allowing them to survive and function effectively in their respective environments.It's important to note that while animal cells lack a cell wall, they still possess a cell membrane (plasma membrane) that separates the cell's internal environment from the external environment. The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier that controls the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.text: Which of the following are related to the synthesis of excretory protein by the cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell membranes, also known as plasma membranes, are the outer boundary of the cell and are involved in various cellular processes. However, they are not directly related to the synthesis of excretory proteins by the cell. Cell membranes primarily serve functions such as maintaining cell integrity, regulating the passage of substances in and out of the cell, and cell signalling.

The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is embedded with ribosomes, which are responsible for the synthesis of proteins. These proteins are then transported through the RER to be modified, folded, and sorted before being transported to their final destination. Some of the proteins synthesized by the RER are excretory proteins secreted by the cell to remove waste products from the body. Therefore, the RER plays a key role in the cell's synthesis and secretion of excretory proteins by the cells.

The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is involved in various cellular functions, including lipid metabolism, detoxification, and calcium storage. However, it is not directly related to the synthesis of excretory proteins. Unlike the rough ER, the smooth ER does not have ribosomes on its surface and is not primarily involved in protein synthesis.

Centrioles are cylindrical structures found in animal cells and are involved in cell division. They play a role in organizing the microtubules that form the spindle apparatus during cell division. Centrioles are not directly related to the synthesis of excretory proteins by the cell.

This option suggests that both cell membranes and the rough endoplasmic reticulum (ER) are related to the synthesis of excretory proteins by the cell. While cell membranes are not directly involved in protein synthesis, the rough ER, with its associated ribosomes, is primarily responsible for protein synthesis. Therefore, this option is not entirely accurate.

questions.explanation: The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is embedded with ribosomes, which are responsible for the synthesis of proteins. These proteins are then transported through the RER to be modified, folded, and sorted before being transported to their final destination. Some of the proteins synthesized by the RER are excretory proteins, which are secreted by the cell to remove waste products from the body. Therefore, the RER plays a key role in the synthesis and secretion of excretory proteins by the cell.

questions.text: Which of these alphabets indicate the presence of the power house of the cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: "M" represents smooth endoplasmatic reticulum. It is a membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells, which lack ribosomes on its surface, giving it a "smooth" appearance under a microscope. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum is involved in lipid metabolism, detoxification, calcium storage, and hormone synthesis. It plays a crucial role in maintaining cellular homeostasis and is an essential organelle for various cellular processes.

"K" represents the nucleolus. The nucleolus is a distinct substructure found within the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. It is a non-membrane-bound organelle composed of proteins and ribonucleic acid (RNA).It plays a crucial role in ribosome biogenesis, assembly of ribosomal subunits, regulation of cell growth and proliferation, and other cellular processes.

C represents the Rough Endoplasmatic reticulum. The rough endoplasmic reticulum is a network of interconnected membranous sacs and tubules that extend throughout the cell's cytoplasm. The presence of ribosomes on its surface gives it a "rough" appearance under a microscope. The rough endoplasmic reticulum works closely with other cellular structures and organelles, such as the Golgi apparatus and the transport vesicles, to ensure proper protein synthesis, folding, modification, and trafficking within the cell.

The alphabet D represents the mitochondria, known as the cell's powerhouse due to its role in producing energy in the form of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through cellular respiration. The mitochondria have a unique double membrane structure and their DNA, which is inherited maternally. They are found in most eukaryotic cells, and their number and size can vary depending on the cell's energy needs.

"E" represents the Golgi apparatus. The Golgi apparatus consists of a series of flattened membrane-bound sacs called cisternae. These cisternae are stacked on each other, with a distinct cis (forming) and trans (maturing) face. The Golgi apparatus is typically located near the nucleus, close to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), from which it receives transport vesicles. The Golgi apparatus plays a central role in the processing, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids within eukaryotic cells.

questions.explanation: The alphabet D represents the mitochondria, which is also known as the cell's powerhouse due to its role in producing energy in the form of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) through cellular respiration. The mitochondria have a unique double membrane structure and their DNA, which is inherited maternally. They are found in most eukaryotic cells, and their number and size can vary depending on the cell's energy needs.

questions.text: The power of a microscope by which we can distinguish two closely located points is termed as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Magnification refers to a microscope's ability to enlarge an object's size, making it appear larger than its actual size. It is the ratio of the apparent size of an object seen through the microscope to its actual size. Magnification helps visualise small details and structures that are invisible to the naked eye. However, magnification alone does not determine the clarity or ability to distinguish closely located points.

Magnification refers to the increase in the apparent size of an object, while resolution refers to the ability to distinguish two separate points as distinct from each other. In other words, the resolution is the power of a microscope to reveal fine detail and distinguish between two closely spaced objects as separate.

Fixation refers to the process of preserving biological specimens for microscopic examination. It involves treating the specimen with chemicals or other methods to stabilize its structure, prevent decay, and maintain its natural properties. Fixation helps to prevent artifacts and maintain the specimen's integrity during the microscopy process, but it does not relate to the ability to distinguish closely located points.

Electrophoresis is a laboratory technique that separates and analyses molecules, such as proteins or nucleic acids, based on their size and charge. It involves applying an electric field to the molecules, causing them to migrate through a gel or other supporting medium. Electrophoresis is not directly related to the power of a microscope to distinguish closely located points.

questions.explanation: Magnification refers to the increase in the apparent size of an object, while resolution refers to the ability to distinguish two different points as distinct from each other. In other words, the resolution is the power of a microscope to reveal fine detail and distinguish between two closely spaced objects as separate.Fixation, electrophoresis, and are techniques used in cell biology for various purposes, but they are not related to the power of a microscope.

questions.text: Sarcoplasmic reticulum is like endoplasmic reticulum but devoid of _ and exhibit a highly specialized _ pattern.

options.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized type of endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells. It is responsible for storing and releasing calcium ions necessary for muscle contraction. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is similar to the endoplasmic reticulum in structure but lacks ribosomes (hence the answer A). The pattern of the sarcoplasmic reticulum is highly specialized and repeating, which allows for the efficient release of calcium ions during muscle contraction.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum is not structurally similar to mitochondria, which are responsible for energy production in cells. Mitochondria are not a part of the sarcoplasmic reticulum and do not exhibit a repeating pattern like the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum lacks ribosomes, which are involved in protein synthesis. Its structure is specialized and different from the endoplasmic reticulum, but it is not described as "simplified." The sarcoplasmic reticulum's primary function is to store and release calcium ions in muscle cells.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum is not comparable to mitochondria in structure or function. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is specialized for calcium ion storage and release, while mitochondria are involved in energy production through cellular respiration.
Ribosome, zig-zag. The sarcoplasmic reticulum does not have a zig-zag pattern. Instead, it exhibits a specialized and repeating pattern to facilitate its function in calcium ion storage and release.

It is responsible for storing and releasing calcium ions necessary for muscle contraction. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is similar to the endoplasmic reticulum in structure but lacks ribosomes (hence the answer A). The pattern of the sarcoplasmic reticulum is highly specialized and repeating, which allows for the efficient release of calcium ions during muscle contraction.

questions.text: Which of the following are the most prominent part of cell membranes in living organisms?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, terpenoids are not the most prominent part of cell membranes. They are found in various parts of organisms and play diverse roles, but they do not predominantly make up cell membranes.
No, waxes are not the most prominent part of cell membranes. While they offer protective functions on the surface of some membranes, they do not form the main structure of the membrane itself.
Correct! Phospholipids are the most prominent part of cell membranes. Their unique structure allows them to form the lipid bilayer, which is fundamental to membrane integrity and function.
No, acylglycerols, such as triglycerides, are primarily energy storage molecules and do not form the main structural component of cell membranes.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Phospholipids are the most prominent and essential component of cell membranes, forming the lipid bilayer that provides structure and function. The amphipathic nature of phospholipids, having hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails, allows them to arrange themselves into a bilayer that acts as a selective barrier. Other options like terpenoids, waxes, and acylglycerols play different roles in organisms but do not form the primary structural framework of cell membranes.

The Golgi complex also has a maturing face, which is the area where the proteins and lipids undergo further processing and modification before they are packaged and transported to their final destinations.

questions.explanation: The Golgi complex is an organelle in the eukaryotic cell that is responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids for delivery to their final destinations. The Golgi complex has two faces, the cis face and the trans face. The inner concave surface of the Golgi complex is the trans face, which is responsible for receiving and sorting newly synthesized proteins and lipids. The outer convex surface is the cis face, which is responsible for packaging and delivering these molecules to their final destinations. The Golgi complex also has a maturing face, which is the area where the proteins and lipids undergo further processing and modification before they are packaged and transported to their final destinations.

questions.text: The process by which various components of cells including its organelle can be isolated is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Homogenization is the process of breaking down cells into their component parts. It involves grinding cells in a suitable medium with the correct pH, ionic composition, temperature, and certain enzymes. This can be done using physical methods, such as blending, or chemical methods, such as using detergents.

Cell fractionation is a multi-step process that typically involves homogenization, followed by centrifugation to separate the cell components by their density. The different cell components can then be further purified using additional methods, such as gel electrophoresis or ultracentrifugation.

Cell fixation is the process of preserving cells so that they can be examined under a microscope. This is typically done by treating cells with a chemical fixative, such as formaldehyde or glutaraldehyde.
Gel electrophoresis is a technique used to separate molecules based on their size and charge. This can be used to separate DNA, RNA, or proteins.
Ultracentrifugation is a technique that uses high centrifugal force to separate molecules based on their density. This can be used to isolate organelles, such as mitochondria or nuclei.

questions.explanation: Option A: Homogenization is a process used in cell fractionation where cells are broken open to release their contents. It involves methods like mechanical disruption, sonication, or enzymatic digestion. Homogenization helps in breaking down the cell structure to release cellular components for further separation and analysis. Option B: Cell fractionation describes the process which is used to to separate individual cell components while maintaining the individual functions of each component. This enables each of the components or organelles to be isolated separately and examined. It involves homogenization first so that the organelles are released from the cells, and the organelles are separated via centrifugation. Hence, this term covers the entire process involved in isolating organelles, so it is the correct answer.Option C: Cell fixation is the preservation of cells or tissues using chemical agents. It involves treating cells with fixatives to immobilize their structures and preserve their components. Fixation helps maintain cellular morphology and prevents degradation or changes in cellular components during subsequent processing steps. Option D: Gel electrophoresis is a technique used to separate and analyze biomolecules, such as proteins or nucleic acids, based on their size and charge. The molecules are placed in a gel matrix and subjected to an electric field. They migrate through the gel at different rates based on their size and charge, allowing for their separation.Option E: Ultracentrifugation is a high-speed centrifugation method used to separate particles based on their density or size. Samples are spun at very high speeds using ultracentrifuges, generating high centrifugal forces. This causes particles to sediment based on their density or size gradients. Ultracentrifugation is commonly used in cell fractionation to separate cellular components, like organelles, based on their density. This is a step involved in the whole cell fractionation process, so option B is the preferred answer over this.

questions.text: Which of the following terms is used to describe the membrane of central vacuole?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term tonoplast is used to describe the membrane of the central vacuole is "Tonoplast." The tonoplast is a specialized membrane found in plant cells that surrounds the central vacuole. It is a selective barrier that regulates the movement of ions, nutrients, and water into and out of the vacuole. The tonoplast is involved in maintaining the osmotic balance and turgor pressure of the cell and plays a crucial role in various cellular processes such as storage, detoxification, and maintaining cell shape.
Myoplast refers to the muscle tissue or the structure and function of muscles. It is not directly related to the membrane of the central vacuole.
Periplast is the outer membrane of a cell. It is made up of a phospholipid bilayer and is responsible for maintaining the cell's shape and protecting it from the environment.

Epitonoplast is a term that is not used in biology. It is a combination of the words "tonoplast" and "epi", which means "on top of". However, there is no such thing as an "epitonoplast". The membrane of the central vacuole is called the tonoplast, and there is no membrane that sits on top of it.

questions.explanation: Option A: Tonoplast is the membrane that surrounds the central vacuole in plant cells. It is a single layer of phospholipids that is selectively permeable, allowing certain molecules to pass through while preventing others from doing so. This allows the central vacuole to maintain a different composition of solutes than the rest of the cell. Option B: Periplast is the outer membrane of a cell. It maintains the cell's shape and protects it from the environment. Option C: Myoplast is a term used to refer to the mitochondria in muscle cells. It produces energy for the cell. Option D: Epitonoplast is a non-existent term. There is no membrane that sits on top of the tonoplast.

questions.text: The outermost boundary in most of the leaf cell is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outermost boundary in most leaf cells is the cell wall. It is a rigid layer that surrounds the cell membrane and provides support and protection for the cell. It is made up of cellulose.
The cell membrane is a thin, flexible layer that surrounds all cells.
The tonoplast is the membrane that surrounds the vacuole, which is a large organelle found in plant cells.

A unit membrane is a single layer of phospholipids that forms the basic structure of all cell membranes. It is made up of the same components as the cell membrane, but it does not form a complete barrier around the cell. Instead, it forms a series of interconnected sheets that allow for the passage of substances between different parts of the cell.

Polar substances are molecules that have a positive and negative end. They are not boundaries of cells, but they can interact with the cell membrane. Polar substances can dissolve in water, and they can also pass through the cell membrane by diffusion.

questions.explanation: Option A: The outermost boundary of most leaf cells is the cell wall. It is a rigid layer that surrounds the cell membrane and provides support and protection for the cell. It is made up of cellulose, a long-chain carbohydrate. The cell wall is not present in animal cells. Option B: The cell membrane is the outermost boundary of all cells, but it is not the outermost boundary of most leaf cells. Option C: The tonoplast is the membrane that surrounds the vacuole, which is a large organelle found in plant cells. It is not the outermost boundary of the cell. Option D: A unit membrane is a single layer of phospholipids that forms the basic structure of all cell membranes. It is not the outermost boundary of any cell. Option E: Polar substances are molecules that have a positive and negative end. They are not boundaries of cells.

questions.text: Role of lysosomes include:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: One of the important functions of the lysosomes is to store hydrolytic enzymes.

Lysosomes are involved in the process of autophagy which is a cellular process involving the degradation and recycling of cellular components, such as damaged organelles or proteins, to maintain cellular homeostasis.
Lysosomes are involved in the destruction of cells through a process called lysosomal cell death or lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP).
All the options stated above are all correct about lysosomes.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes store hydrolytic enzymes, which play their role in autophagy and destruction of old cells. Hence, the correct option is D.

questions.text: In higher animals' bodies, tissue fluid is isotonic contrary to plants because in plant cells:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Cell membrane creates resistance in water uptake
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While the cell membrane regulates the movement of substances in and out of cells, it does not create resistance to water uptake in plant cells; that role is fulfilled by the cell wall.

options.text: Plastids create resistance in water uptake
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Plastids, including chloroplasts, are involved in processes like photosynthesis and storage. They do not influence water uptake or create resistance to it.

options.text: Chlorophyll creates resistance in water uptake
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Chlorophyll is the pigment responsible for photosynthesis and does not have any role in water uptake or resistance.

options.text: Cell wall creates resistance in water uptake
options.explanation: This option is correct. The cell wall in plant cells exerts a pressure potential that helps balance osmotic potential, thus creating resistance to excessive water uptake, unlike the situation in animal cells.

questions.explanation: In plant cells, the presence of a rigid cell wall creates pressure potential, which contributes to the overall osmotic balance by exerting resistance against excessive water uptake. This prevents the cells from becoming plasmolyzed or turgid beyond a certain point. In contrast, animal cells lack such a structure, relying instead on isotonic conditions to maintain equilibrium. As a result, tissue fluid in higher animals is isotonic to prevent osmotic imbalance. The incorrect options confuse the role of different organelles and components; the cell membrane, plastids, and chlorophyll do not contribute to water uptake resistance as the cell wall does.

questions.text: What type of protein is present in eukaryotic DNA but NOT in prokaryotic DNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Receptor proteins are involved in cell communication and signal transduction, not in DNA packaging.
Glycoproteins have carbohydrates attached and play roles in cell-cell interactions and immune responses, not in DNA packaging.
Chromatids are structures formed during cell division and are not specific proteins involved in DNA packaging.
Histones are proteins that eukaryotic cells use to organize and compact DNA into chromatin, a function not typically found in prokaryotic cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Histone protein. In eukaryotic cells, DNA is wrapped around histone proteins to form chromatin, aiding in the efficient packaging and regulation of DNA. Most prokaryotic cells do not possess histones, with some exceptions like certain Archaea. Receptor proteins, glycoproteins, and chromatid proteins are not involved in DNA organization in this manner. Receptor proteins are for signaling, glycoproteins are for cell communication, and chromatids are DNA structures, not proteins.

questions.text: A group of biologically active molecules formed from amino acids which interact with the surfiace of the lipid bilayer of cell membranes are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Integral proteins are embedded within the lipid bilayer and can span across it. They interact with both the hydrophobic core and the hydrophilic exterior of the membrane, unlike peripheral proteins which only interact with the surface.

Peripheral proteins are loosely attached to the surface of the cell membrane, interacting with the lipid bilayer or integral proteins. They play crucial roles in signaling and cellular processes without penetrating the hydrophobic core.

The cell wall is a rigid structure found in plant, bacterial, fungal, and some protist cells, providing support and protection. It is not involved in the interaction with the lipid bilayer of animal cell membranes.
Plasmodesmata are channels between plant cells allowing communication and transport. They are not proteins interacting with the lipid bilayer surface.

questions.explanation: Peripheral proteins, or peripheral membrane proteins, are biologically active molecules formed from amino acids that interact with the surface of the lipid bilayer of cell membranes. Unlike integral membrane proteins, they do not embed within the hydrophobic core of the membrane, but instead attach to the exterior surfaces, often through interactions with integral proteins or the polar heads of lipids. Integral proteins, by contrast, penetrate the lipid bilayer; the cell wall is a structural feature, and plasmodesmata facilitate intercellular communication in plants.

questions.text: While working in a laboratory, before studying a sample under the microscope, it was immersed in a dye solution to obtain _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Magnification refers to the process of enlarging the appearance of an object, which is achieved by using the optical components of a microscope, not by immersing a sample in dye.
This option is incorrect. An image is the visual representation of an object as seen through the microscope, but dye immersion is not specifically used to create an image.
This option is incorrect. Matching refers to comparing similarities, which is unrelated to the process of using dyes for sample preparation.

This option is correct. Contrast is enhanced by dyes or stains, making different structures within the sample more distinguishable under the microscope. This allows for clearer observation and differentiation between various cell components.

questions.explanation: Staining is used to enhance contrast in microscopic samples, making it easier to distinguish between different structures and components within the sample. By increasing contrast, stains help highlight specific features that might otherwise be difficult to see. The other options are unrelated to the purpose of using dyes: magnification is achieved by the microscope's lenses, images are the visual outcomes, and matching is a comparison process, not related to the dye's function.

questions.text: When a plant cell receives a signal for death, it commits suicide by rupturing:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus does not rupture during programmed cell death in plants. It is primarily involved in storing genetic material and regulating cellular activities, not directly in the process of cell death.
The cell membrane maintains the integrity of the cell structure but is not the primary site of rupture during programmed cell death. The key rupture occurs in the tonoplast, not the cell membrane.
The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole in plant cells. During programmed cell death, the tonoplast ruptures, releasing vacuolar contents, including enzymes, which lead to cell death.

Chloroplasts are involved in photosynthesis and are not responsible for the rupture that leads to cell death. They have a double-membrane structure and do not play a direct role in programmed cell death.

questions.explanation: In plant cells, programmed cell death (PCD) is an orderly process that involves the rupture of the tonoplast, the membrane surrounding the vacuole. This rupture releases vacuolar contents, including enzymes, into the cell, leading to its demise. The tonoplast's rupture is crucial as it allows the enzymes to interact with other cellular components, leading to cell death. The nucleus, cell membrane, and chloroplasts are not involved in this specific rupture process. The nucleus manages genetic information, the cell membrane maintains cellular integrity, and chloroplasts function in photosynthesis, none of which directly cause cell death through rupturing in PCD.

questions.text: Cell permeability and transport processes of cell membrane depend upon its _ component.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The permeability of the cell membrane and its permeability depends upon the phospholipid content of that membrane.

This option is not correct. Carbohydrates are present in the cell membrane in the form of glycolipids and glycoproteins. These molecules are involved in cell recognition, cell signaling, and immunity.
Polysaccharides are not typically found in the cell membrane.

Cellulose is a polysaccharide that is found in the cell walls of plants. It provides structural support and protection to the cell, but it is not directly involved in the permeability or transport processes of the cell membrane.

questions.explanation: The permeability of the cell membrane and its permeability depends upon the phospholipid content of that membrane. All other chemical components mentioned (carbohydrates, polysaccharides, and cellulose) are not part of the cell membrane.

questions.text: Label the following phenomenon
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The diagram shows phagocytosis, which is the process by which a cell uses its plasma membrane to engulf a large particle, giving rise to an internal compartment called the phagosome. It is one type of endocytosis.Exocytosis is a form of active transport and bulk transport in which a cell transports molecules out of the cell.
